title: Executive Order 12864—United States Advisory Council on the National Information Infrastructure

flags.justification: The Secretary's authority to appoint up to 25 members and designate chairs (Sec. 1) is standard, unremarkable Federal Advisory Committee Act structure common across advisory bodies in this corpus; this alone is not a meaningful concentration finding.
The Council is purely advisory with no operational deliverables to fail at; 'no defined metrics for success' is the normal, expected shape of an advisory body's mandate, not an accountability gap.

The ten enumerated issue areas (Sec. 2(b)) are broad but are facets of one coherent domain — national information infrastructure policy — not distinct, unrelated problem frames forced under a single framing.
An advisory body has no compliance target or enforcement mechanism to be vague about.

The genuine incentive concern here — industry-affiliated members advising on policy affecting their own industry without standard ethics constraints — is a specific, named exemption best captured under Flag 11 rather than double-counted here as a general incentive finding.
Sec. 4(b) sets an explicit two-year existence limit 'unless the Council's charter is subsequently extended' — a genuine, well-designed sunset/reauthorization mechanism.

Sec. 4(a) delegates most presidential FACA oversight functions down to the Secretary, though it explicitly and specifically preserves the most externally meaningful check: 'except that of reporting to Congress.' A partial, not complete, reduction in oversight seniority.
This is a policy-advisory body, not a compliance regime with 'violations' for a third party to surface; the concept does not map.

The Council's advisory scope spans commerce, national security, privacy, copyright, health care, and education (Sec. 2(b)) for a major, then-unsettled emerging policy domain — real agenda-setting influence beyond a narrow technical advisory role.
Sec. 3(a)'s directive that agency heads 'provide... such information as it may require' is an information-request cooperation clause, not a redirection of another agency's operational mandate.

Sec. 4(c) exempts Council and subcommittee members from being considered special government employees 'for any purpose,' specifically including a named list of federal conflict-of-interest and ethics statutes (18 U.S.C. §§ 201-203, 205, 207-209, 218-219). This is a broad, specifically-enumerated exemption from ethics law for a body advising on regulatory and commercial policy directly affecting the industries its own members likely represent — exactly the exemption-architecture failure mode the flag targets.

notes: Reconciliation pass (2026-07-04). Structural weight: 4/18 = 22.2%, NOT SIGNIFICANT — sides with Gemini's classification over ChatGPT's, despite agreeing with ChatGPT that Flag 11 is a genuine, well-supported CRITICAL finding (the ethics exemption is real and specific, not overreach). ChatGPT's other PRESENT calls (Flags 1, 2, 3, 5, 8) largely describe normal FACA advisory-committee structure rather than distinguishing structural problems; disagreeing with those keeps the aggregate below threshold even with one confirmed severe finding. Illustrates a real property of the normalized-average design: a single strong, correctly-identified CRITICAL flag does not by itself make an 11-dimension aggregate cross the significance threshold.
